recipes.title: Add a project feature
steps: Name the feature's authoritative state, behavior, scene bridge, and external dependencies.
Place the feature in one declared project module outside addons/gf.
Use a GF Model, System, Utility, or Controller only for the responsibility it owns.
Register long-lived architecture members from the composition root; keep scene objects scene-owned.
Test observable state transitions and lifecycle cleanup.
avoid: One class per architecture role without responsibility
Business fields added to GF resources
Scene nodes retained by long-lived services

recipes.title: Compose project modules and adapters
steps: Create a project-owned GFInstaller as the composition root.
Select concrete services and provider adapters from contract constraints.
Register stable protocols before dependent systems initialize.
Keep feature flow and SDK callbacks out of the installer.
Verify initialization, disposal, and replacement in tests.
avoid: Service locator calls scattered through domain code
Platform checks spread across systems

recipes.title: Implement an owned async operation
steps: Choose the node, service, or request that owns the operation.
Define success, failure, cancellation, and timeout terminal states.
Bind cancellation to owner disposal or scene exit.
Normalize callbacks or signals into one completion boundary.
Observe every terminal state and test late callbacks.
avoid: Fire-and-forget work with hidden failure
Awaiting a signal whose emitter can disappear

recipes.title: Load a resource with explicit ownership
steps: Resolve a stable resource key or catalog entry instead of scattering paths.
Acquire through GFAssetUtility with an owner, group, or explicit release policy.
Handle cancellation and load failure before exposing the resource.
Release the handle when the owner ends.
Test cache and owner-disposal behavior.
avoid: Unowned long-lived cache pins
Runtime content identity based only on raw res:// paths

recipes.title: Compile project configuration
steps: Keep business schema in project-owned source and schema files.
Use Reader, Layout, Validation, Target, and Commit stages as separate concerns.
Inspect the versioned IR before materialization when adding custom targets.
Fail closed on duplicate identities, invalid references, and partial commits.
Verify freshness fingerprints and rollback.
avoid: Project columns hardcoded into GF stages
Writing outputs before all semantic validation passes

recipes.title: Define persistence and save ownership
steps: Declare persistence mode and compatibility guarantees in the project contract.
Choose local dictionary storage or scene save graph by ownership shape.
Assign stable identities and project-owned serializers or data sources.
Make migrations explicit and transactional.
Test corruption, interruption, old versions, and missing entities.
avoid: Persisting process-local IDs
Treating encryption, integrity, and obfuscation as equivalent

recipes.title: Adapt a platform lobby provider
steps: Define provider-neutral identity, lobby descriptor, member, invite, and error records in project or adapter code.
Keep Steam, Epic, console, or service SDK calls inside the adapter.
Normalize initialization, capability checks, callbacks, cancellation, and provider shutdown.
Let project code own lobby visibility, matchmaking policy, UI, and fallback.
Test unavailable SDK, late callbacks, disconnect, ownership transfer, and duplicate events.
avoid: Provider IDs leaking into portable domain models
A GFSteamManager-style global business facade

recipes.title: Adapt cloud or platform storage
steps: Implement provider calls behind GFStorageBackend or a project-owned equivalent.
Normalize keys, revisions, availability, errors, and cancellation.
Declare conflict and offline policy in project code.
Keep credentials and account data outside logs and feedback drafts.
Test stale revisions, partial failures, quota, offline recovery, and retries.
avoid: Cloud policy embedded in generic storage
Last-write-wins without an explicit project decision

recipes.title: Implement a replaceable provider backend
steps: Select the narrow GF protocol that matches the provider capability.
Declare capability support before handling requests.
Translate provider errors and lifecycle into stable project-facing results.
Fallback only when project policy explicitly permits it.
Contract-test the backend independently from the SDK-facing wrapper.
avoid: Provider-specific methods added to a generic protocol
Silent fallback that changes product behavior

recipes.title: Implement a network transport backend
steps: Keep transport bytes and connection state separate from project messages.
Declare authority, reliability, ordering, and payload limits in project contracts.
Normalize peer identity and disconnect reasons at the adapter boundary.
Use GF network contracts for stable payloads when appropriate.
Test loss, duplication, reordering, reconnect, oversized payloads, and shutdown.
avoid: Transport callbacks mutating gameplay state directly
Assuming a transport implies authority policy

recipes.title: Escalate a project finding to GF
steps: Minimize the failure outside project business flow.
Classify project misuse, framework defect, documentation gap, reusable mechanism, or adapter gap.
Create a structured candidate with expected, actual, reproduction, environment, impact, and workaround.
Generate a redacted draft and inspect the exact payload.
Check duplicates and submit only after explicit approval of the payload hash.
avoid: Uploading raw project files or logs
Submitting speculative ideas as confirmed defects

recipes.title: Organize optional project content
steps: Assign stable content package IDs and resource keys.
Keep dependencies acyclic and paths inside each package root.
Let project tooling own download, entitlement, activation, and CDN policy.
Build and audit an export plan before packaging.
Test missing, duplicate, and cyclic dependencies plus unavailable content.
avoid: Download URLs embedded in the runtime content manifest
Gameplay rules keyed to physical file paths

recipes.title: Add input contexts and rebinding
steps: Define project-owned abstract actions and contexts.
Map device events through bindings, modifiers, and triggers.
Use a detector and conflict analysis before committing rebinds.
Persist project rebind policy separately from gameplay state.
Test context overlap, device changes, cancellation, and inaccessible bindings.
avoid: Gameplay code reading physical keys directly
A rebinding UI silently resolving conflicts

recipes.title: Compose a UI screen flow
steps: Separate logical navigation layers from CanvasLayer drawing order.
Represent screen intent in project-owned navigation requests and state.
Bind controls to reactive state without placing business decisions in views.
Define modal, replace, hide-under, focus, and back behavior explicitly.
Test rapid transitions, scene disposal, focus restoration, and duplicate requests.
avoid: Cross-layer cleanup assumed from replace
Global UI state embedded in individual controls

recipes.title: Choose state machine, flow, or action queue
steps: Use a state machine for mutually exclusive durable modes.
Use a flow graph for inspectable progression through heterogeneous steps.
Use an action queue for ordered or parallel transient work, especially presentation.
Assign one owner for cancellation and terminal reporting.
Test interruption, loops, missing targets, and owner disposal.
avoid: Nesting orchestrators to model the same state
Using a visual queue as authoritative domain state

recipes.title: Publish bounded diagnostics
steps: Choose the minimum fields needed to answer a concrete operational question.
Publish a bounded, already-sanitized snapshot through an owner-bound contribution.
Keep callbacks out of hot snapshot collection paths.
Define retention, cardinality, and text budgets.
Test disabled collection, owner disposal, oversized values, and secret redaction.
avoid: Unbounded histories
Precise device, account, scene, or path data collected by default

recipes.title: Define a deterministic simulation scope
steps: Declare which inputs, state, ticks, and outputs must reproduce.
Use fixed GF random state and canonical supported value types.
Exclude or quantize nondeterministic physics, clocks, floats, and provider callbacks.
Record seeds and canonical state hashes.
Verify golden sequences across clean runs and target platforms.
avoid: Assuming Godot RNG implementation is a permanent wire contract
Hashing unordered or unsupported Variant graphs

recipes.title: Use a reusable domain component
steps: Select a component only for generic state and operations it already owns.
Keep item meaning, rewards, economy, balance, and progression policy in project systems.
Store project-specific fields in documented metadata or project-owned resources.
Define save and replication boundaries explicitly.
Test invalid IDs, limits, duplicate operations, and migration.
avoid: Subclassing generic data to hide an entire gameplay system
Adding one game's vocabulary to GF component code

recipes.title: Build a dialogue pipeline
steps: Compile strict project-owned source into GF dialogue resources at authoring time.
Provide condition, mutation, and text resolution callbacks from project context.
Keep UI, localization tables, characters, quests, and rewards outside the runner.
Persist stable line and response identities when resume is required.
Test missing targets, cycles, invalid mutations, cancellation, and source diagnostics.
avoid: Business mutation names hardcoded into the compiler
Dialogue UI owning authoritative quest state

recipes.title: Apply spatial or physics mechanisms
steps: Separate pure spatial math or sampling from world ownership and node mutation.
Declare coordinate, units, layer, update cadence, and authority contracts.
Let the project choose candidates, probe placement, force application, and collision policy.
Bound queries and incremental work.
Test empty worlds, invalid values, large coordinates, frame-rate variation, and disposal.
avoid: Physics ownership hidden inside a math helper
Unbounded world scans on frame callbacks
